title: What Midden Is
text: Midden is Cairn Systems' content-addressable storage engine, used as the backing object store beneath several of Cairn's backup, artifact-repository, and build-cache products since its first production release in 2019. Every object Midden stores is identified by the cryptographic hash of its own content rather than by a path or an arbitrarily assigned identifier, so two callers that write byte-for-byte identical data always produce the same stored object regardless of whether they coordinated with each other in advance. This content-addressed identity is what makes deduplication essentially free at the storage layer: Midden never needs a separate deduplication pass, since any two identical pieces of content naturally hash to the same address and are stored, by construction, exactly once. Large objects are split into smaller chunks before hashing, using a content-defined chunking scheme rather than fixed-size splitting, a distinction that turns out to matter enormously for deduplication effectiveness on data that shifts slightly between versions.

title: Content Hashing
text: Midden hashes every chunk using a 256-bit BLAKE3-family digest, chosen for its high single-core throughput relative to older cryptographic hash functions still common in comparable systems. On Cairn's reference hardware, a single core hashes content at approximately 3.2 gigabytes per second, and Midden's chunking and hashing pipeline parallelizes across available cores, reaching an aggregate 22 gigabytes per second on a 16-core ingest node during Cairn's standard throughput benchmark. Digest collisions have never been observed across Midden's production deployment, which as of the most recent audit has stored roughly 940 million distinct chunk objects. Each stored chunk carries a 32-byte digest, a 4-byte length field, and a compressed-size field, for 44 bytes of fixed metadata overhead per chunk regardless of chunk size.

title: Content-Defined Chunking
text: Rather than splitting an incoming object into fixed-size blocks, Midden scans its content with a rolling hash function, computing a new hash value incrementally as a sliding window advances one byte at a time, and declares a chunk boundary whenever the rolling hash's low bits match a configured pattern. This content-defined approach means chunk boundaries depend only on local content, not on the object's absolute byte offset, so inserting or deleting a few bytes near the start of a large file shifts only the chunk containing that edit, leaving every chunk boundary elsewhere in the file completely unchanged. Fixed-size chunking, by contrast, would shift every single chunk boundary after the edit point, destroying deduplication against a previous version of the same file for any edit that isn't a clean append at the very end. This locality property is the entire reason content-defined chunking exists, and it is what lets Midden deduplicate effectively against near-identical, rather than only byte-identical, prior objects.

title: Chunk Size Distribution
text: Midden's default rolling-hash configuration targets an average chunk size of 64 kilobytes, with a hard minimum of 16 kilobytes and a hard maximum of 256 kilobytes to bound both per-chunk metadata overhead and worst-case chunk size when the rolling hash fails to find a boundary for an unusually long stretch. Measured across Cairn's aggregated backup workload telemetry, actual chunk sizes cluster with a median of 61 kilobytes and a standard deviation of 34 kilobytes, closely tracking the theoretical geometric distribution the rolling-hash boundary condition produces. Roughly 2.1 percent of chunks hit the 256-kilobyte maximum bound rather than finding a natural boundary, most commonly within highly repetitive or low-entropy content such as sparse files or padded binary formats.

title: Chunking Profile Comparison
text:
Midden supports three preset chunking profiles trading chunk-metadata overhead against deduplication granularity, summarized below.

| Profile | Target chunk size | Min / max | Typical dedup ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fine | 16 KB | 4 KB / 64 KB | 3.8x |
| Standard | 64 KB | 16 KB / 256 KB | 3.1x |
| Coarse | 256 KB | 64 KB / 1 MB | 2.4x |

The fine profile achieves the highest deduplication ratio on Cairn's backup workloads but at roughly triple the per-object metadata overhead of the standard profile, a tradeoff most deployments only accept for workloads with unusually small, frequent edits.

title: Deduplication Mechanism
text: Every chunk Midden writes passes through a lookup against its existing chunk store before any data actually hits disk: if a chunk with the same content hash already exists, Midden simply increments that chunk's reference count and discards the newly computed, but redundant, chunk data without writing it. This reference-counted design means storage cost for a given logical object depends only on how many of its chunks are genuinely new relative to everything already stored, not on how many times the object itself has been logically written. A large object is represented not as a single stored blob but as an ordered list of chunk references, each pointing to a chunk that may be shared across many unrelated objects, with the object's own identity ultimately determined by hashing that ordered list of references rather than the raw content directly.

title: Deduplication Ratios
text: Across Cairn's aggregated production telemetry, Midden achieves a median deduplication ratio of 3.4x on incremental backup workloads, meaning roughly 71 percent of chunks in a typical incremental backup already exist in the store from a prior backup of the same source. Virtual-machine disk-image workloads see a lower but still substantial 1.8x ratio, reflecting less inter-version content overlap than typical file-based backups. The single highest observed deduplication ratio in Cairn's production fleet, 47x, occurred on a customer's repeated daily database dumps whose underlying data changed only marginally between exports. Chunk-store lookup for an incoming chunk completes in a median of 80 microseconds against a fully warmed index covering the reference hardware's 940-million-chunk store.

title: Rolling-Hash Boundary Condition
text: Midden's chunk-boundary rule declares a boundary at byte position i whenever RollingHash(window_i) AND mask == 0, where mask is a bitmask with exactly log2(target_chunk_size) low bits set, so that a boundary occurs, on average, once every target_chunk_size bytes under the assumption that rolling-hash output bits are effectively uniformly distributed. This gives chunk sizes a geometric distribution with mean equal to target_chunk_size, which is why Midden separately enforces hard minimum and maximum bounds rather than relying on the geometric distribution's tail alone, since an unbounded geometric distribution would occasionally produce pathologically tiny or pathologically large chunks. Choosing target_chunk_size involves a direct tradeoff: halving it roughly doubles both the number of stored chunks and the fixed per-chunk metadata overhead, while typically improving deduplication ratio by a smaller, workload-dependent margin, which is why Midden's standard 64-kilobyte target represents a deliberately conservative middle point rather than the theoretical dedup-maximizing extreme.

title: Garbage Collection Procedure
text:
Midden's garbage collector runs the following mark-and-sweep sequence to reclaim storage from chunks no longer referenced by any live object. 1. Enumerate every live object root currently referenced by an active namespace or retained snapshot.
2. Walk each root's chunk-reference list, marking every reachable chunk as live in a temporary bitmap.
3. Scan the full chunk store, comparing each stored chunk's identifier against the live bitmap.
4. Queue any chunk absent from the live bitmap for deletion, deferring actual deletion by a configurable grace period.
5. After the grace period elapses, delete queued chunks and reclaim their storage. The grace period in step four exists specifically to protect against a concurrent write that references a chunk the mark phase started just before it became reachable.

title: Prior Research: Rolling-Fingerprint Chunking
text: Content-defined chunking traces back to foundational rolling-hash and fingerprinting research published well before most production deduplication systems existed, including work outside the English-language systems literature. Cohen's 2012 paper, פילוג תוכן לחלקים באמצעות טביעות אצבע מתגלגלות לצורך כפילות אחסון (Content-Defined Splitting Using Rolling Fingerprints for Storage Deduplication), analyzed boundary-detection stability under small content edits across several rolling-hash function families, concluding that a simple modular-mask boundary condition performed comparably to more elaborate alternatives while remaining far cheaper to compute per byte scanned. Cairn's chunking implementation follows this simpler modular-mask approach directly, citing Cohen's stability analysis as justification for not pursuing the more computationally expensive alternatives the paper also evaluated.

title: Merkle-Tree Object References
text: Above the chunk layer, Midden represents every stored object as a Merkle tree whose leaves are chunk hashes and whose internal nodes are hashes of their own children's hashes, so that a single root hash cryptographically commits to the entire object's content, chunk boundaries included. This structure lets Midden verify a large object's integrity without reading the entire object: a caller can request just the root hash and a small proof path to confirm a specific chunk belongs to a specific object, without downloading or hashing every other chunk that object contains. Objects composed of many chunks use an intermediate layer of tree nodes rather than a single flat list of chunk hashes directly under the root, keeping proof-path length logarithmic in chunk count rather than growing linearly with the number of chunks a large object happens to contain.

title: Merkle-Tree Statistics
text: A typical Midden object composed of 1,000 chunks organizes its Merkle tree with a branching factor of 16, producing a tree depth of 3 levels above the chunk-hash leaves. Verifying a single chunk's inclusion against the object's root hash requires hashing a proof path of 3 intermediate nodes, completing in under 12 microseconds on Cairn's reference hardware. The largest single object in Cairn's production fleet, a 340-gigabyte data-warehouse export, decomposes into roughly 5.3 million chunks and a Merkle tree 5 levels deep, with full-object integrity verification, hashing every chunk and every intermediate node, completing in 41 seconds on a single ingest node.

title: GC Performance by Store Size
text:
Garbage collection sweep time scales with total chunk-store size, as measured across four production deployments of varying scale.

| Store size | Chunk count | Mark phase | Sweep phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 TB | 30M chunks | 4 min | 6 min |
| 20 TB | 310M chunks | 38 min | 52 min |
| 90 TB | 940M chunks | 118 min | 160 min |

Sweep-phase time grows slightly faster than linearly with chunk count because larger stores also see proportionally more index-lookup contention from concurrent write traffic occurring during the sweep.

title: Hash-Collision Probability
text: The probability of an accidental hash collision across Midden's chunk store follows the birthday-bound approximation P(collision) approximately equals n^2 / (2 * 2^b), where n is the total number of distinct chunks stored and b is the digest length in bits. Substituting Midden's 256-bit digest and the production fleet's current 940-million-chunk count, n^2 / (2 * 2^b) evaluates to a probability on the order of 10^-61, several dozen orders of magnitude below any physically meaningful risk threshold, which is why Midden's design treats hash collision as effectively impossible rather than engineering explicit collision-detection logic into the write path. This calculation is also why Cairn selected a 256-bit digest rather than a shorter, faster-to-compute alternative: at the fleet's projected decade-scale growth trajectory, even a 128-bit digest would remain astronomically safe, but the marginal hashing-throughput cost of the longer digest was judged negligible against the design-review comfort of an unambiguously overwhelming safety margin.

title: Ingest Benchmark Comparison
text: Cairn benchmarked Midden's ingest throughput against two comparison content-addressable storage systems on an identical 500-gigabyte mixed backup corpus. Midden ingested the corpus in 24 minutes at a sustained 22 gigabytes per second aggregate hashing rate, achieving a 3.1x deduplication ratio on the corpus's standard chunking profile. The first comparison system completed the same ingest in 41 minutes with a 2.9x ratio, and the second completed in 33 minutes with a 3.3x ratio, marginally beating Midden's deduplication ratio but at roughly 35 percent lower ingest throughput. Cairn's internal conclusion was that Midden's throughput advantage, rather than a small deduplication-ratio edge either direction, was the more operationally significant differentiator for its own backup product's daily ingest windows.

title: Prior Research: Hash-Tree Verification
text: Merkle-tree-based integrity verification for large, chunked objects also draws on research published in venues outside the dominant English-language distributed-systems literature. Papadopoulos's 2016 study, Επαλήθευση ακεραιότητας μεγάλων αντικειμένων μέσω δέντρων κατακερματισμού (Integrity Verification of Large Objects Through Hash Trees), analyzed proof-path length as a function of branching factor for chunked-object hash trees, concluding that a branching factor between 8 and 32 minimized total proof-verification cost across realistic object-size distributions better than either a flat structure or a strictly binary tree. Cairn's choice of a 16-way branching factor for Midden's Merkle tree sits squarely inside the range this study identified as near-optimal, a design parameter Cairn's architecture documentation cites the study for directly.

title: Chunking-Profile Migration Checklist
text:
Operators upgrading a Midden deployment to a new chunking-profile default follow this migration sequence to avoid disrupting deduplication against existing stored data. 1. Confirm the target chunking profile's parameters against the deployment's typical object-size distribution using Midden's offline profiling tool.
2. Leave existing stored chunks and their reference counts untouched; do not attempt to re-chunk data already written under the previous profile.
3. Configure new writes to use the new chunking profile going forward, accepting a temporary deduplication-ratio dip against pre-migration data.
4. Monitor deduplication ratio for at least one full backup cycle to confirm it recovers as new writes increasingly compare against other new writes.
5. Update the deployment's documented default profile only after the monitoring period confirms no unexpected ratio regression. Attempting to force re-chunking of existing data has historically caused more operational disruption than the temporary ratio dip this checklist accepts instead.

title: FAQ: Single-Core Hashing Throughput
text: Q: How fast can a single core hash content in Midden? A: On Cairn's reference hardware, a single core hashes content at approximately 2.6 gigabytes per second, and Midden's chunking and hashing pipeline parallelizes across available cores, reaching an aggregate 22 gigabytes per second on a 16-core ingest node during Cairn's standard throughput benchmark. Actual single-core throughput varies somewhat by CPU generation and by whether the deployment's hardware supports the vectorized hash-compression instructions Midden's implementation prefers when available. Deployments running on older hardware lacking these instructions should expect noticeably lower single-core throughput than the reference figure, though aggregate throughput on a sufficiently multi-core node typically still meets most backup workloads' ingest-window requirements.

title: Errata: Q2 2024 Deduplication Benchmark Report
text: Errata to Cairn's Q2 2024 deduplication benchmark report. The originally published report overstated the aggregate incremental-backup deduplication figure due to a corpus-selection error that overrepresented a small number of unusually repetitive customer workloads. The corrected figure follows. Across Cairn's aggregated production telemetry, Midden achieves a median deduplication ratio of 2.9x on incremental backup workloads, meaning roughly 71 percent of chunks in a typical incremental backup already exist in the store from a prior backup of the same source. Customers who sized storage capacity planning around the original 3.4x figure are advised to re-run their capacity projections using this corrected figure, particularly for workloads with lower inherent content overlap between backup generations.

title: Incident Postmortem: The February 2022 GC Race Condition
text: In February 2022, a Cairn-operated Midden deployment supporting a customer's nightly backup pipeline experienced a garbage-collection race condition that deleted a small number of chunks still referenced by an object whose write had not yet completed, the closest Midden's production history has come to an actual data-loss incident rather than a purely theoretical risk. The affected deployment had recently been reconfigured to shorten the garbage collector's deletion grace period from the default 24 hours to 2 hours, a change requested by the customer to reduce storage costs during a period of unusually high write volume, without fully accounting for how the shortened window interacted with the deployment's own backup jobs, which occasionally ran for longer than 2 hours on the customer's largest data sets. A backup job writing a 400-gigabyte object began hashing and storing chunks as usual, but because the job's total runtime exceeded the newly shortened 2-hour grace period, the garbage collector's mark phase ran, failed to find several of the in-progress object's newly written chunks referenced by any completed object root, since the object's root itself had not yet been finalized, and correctly queued those chunks for deletion under its own logic before the write job could complete and establish the reference that would have protected them. Deletion of the queued chunks proceeded after the shortened grace period elapsed, roughly ninety minutes before the backup job finished, at which point the job's final write step failed with a chunk-not-found error rather than silently succeeding, since Midden's object-finalization step verifies every referenced chunk's presence before committing a new root hash. No previously completed object was affected, and no data corruption occurred in any object a caller had ever successfully read; the incident manifested as a failed backup job rather than corrupted backup data, though the failed job did need to be re-run from scratch, doubling that night's ingest time for the affected customer. Cairn's investigation concluded the mark phase's definition of liveness, rooted only in finalized object roots, was correct in isolation but had never been validated against grace periods short enough to plausibly race against a single long-running write job, an interaction the original grace-period design had not anticipated when customers were expected to keep the default 24-hour setting. The remediation added a minimum enforced grace period of 6 hours regardless of customer configuration, along with an explicit warning during configuration that flags any requested grace period shorter than a deployment's own historical maximum backup-job duration. Cairn also added a write-in-progress marker the mark phase now checks directly, protecting actively-writing objects' chunks before their root hash is finalized, closing the specific race this incident exposed rather than relying solely on the minimum grace-period floor.
